steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence reads: "Dana Claxton is a Hunkpapa Lakota artist whose body of [blank] interdisciplinary, encompassing photography, video, and performance..." The relative clause introduced by "whose" needs a finite verb to form a complete predicate before the adjective "interdisciplinary."

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'work, to be': "to be" is an infinitive, not a finite verb. It cannot serve as the main verb of a relative clause. ✗
  • Choice B — 'work is': "is" is a finite verb. The clause reads "whose body of work is interdisciplinary" — a complete, grammatically correct predicate. ✓
  • Choice C — 'work, having been': "having been" is a past participial phrase, not a finite verb. It cannot anchor the relative clause. ✗
  • Choice D — 'work being': "being" is a present participle here, not a finite verb. The clause has no main verb. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
